events.title: Military Coup Ousts Civilian Government
events.detail: SAF Commander Gen. Abdel Fattah al-Burhan and RSF Chief Gen. Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo (Hemedti) jointly led a coup against the transitional civilian government, arresting Prime Minister Abdalla Hamdok and dissolving the Sovereignty Council. The coup was widely condemned internationally and triggered mass pro-democracy protests across Sudan.

events.title: Hamdok Reinstated, Then Resigns
events.detail: Under international pressure, the military reached a deal reinstating PM Hamdok, but he resigned on January 2, 2022, saying the deal had failed to restore the democratic transition. Sudan descended into a prolonged period of military rule with no civilian government.

events.title: RSF Formally Created from Janjaweed Militias
events.detail: President Omar al-Bashir formalized the Rapid Support Forces as a standing paramilitary institution in 2013, drawing from Janjaweed Arab militias responsible for the 2003–2010 Darfur genocide. Hemedti (Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo) was appointed RSF commander. This institutionalization laid the foundation for the RSF's later rivalry with the SAF and repeated patterns of ethnic targeting in Darfur.

events.title: Omar al-Bashir Overthrown in Military Coup
events.detail: Following months of mass civilian protests against his 30-year rule, President Omar al-Bashir was overthrown in a joint military-RSF coup. A Transitional Sovereignty Council was formed with both military and civilian members, beginning Sudan's fragile democratic transition. Both Burhan (SAF) and Hemedti (RSF) emerged as dominant figures, sidelining civilian voices from the start.

events.title: Political Framework Agreement Signed
events.detail: SAF and RSF signed a political framework agreement with civilian forces to restore Sudan's transition to democracy, setting a two-year timeline for handing power to civilians. The agreement was seen as a precursor to a formal constitutional declaration but underlying tensions over RSF integration into the army remained unresolved.

events.title: RSF Deploys Near Merowe, Tensions Peak
events.detail: RSF forces deployed near the city of Merowe and in Khartoum without SAF authorization. The SAF ordered them to withdraw; the RSF refused. The disputed timeline for integrating RSF troops into the SAF within 2 years (vs. RSF's demand for 10 years) had reached a breaking point. Both sides began positioning troops for a potential showdown.

events.title: War Breaks Out in Khartoum
events.detail: At approximately 9 AM local time, the RSF launched coordinated attacks on SAF positions across Khartoum, Omdurman, and Bahri. RSF fighters stormed Khartoum International Airport, the Presidential Palace, and state television headquarters. Simultaneous clashes erupted in El Fasher, Nyala, El Geneina, El Obeid, and Kassala. Both sides accused the other of firing first.

events.title: RSF Storms Gen. Burhan's Residence
events.detail: RSF fighters stormed SAF commander Gen. Abdel Fattah al-Burhan's Khartoum residence in an apparent assassination attempt. Burhan and his bodyguards fought back; a bodyguard reported Burhan personally fired an AK-47 at attackers. Burhan escaped but over 30 bodyguards were killed. The RSF also claimed to have taken the airport and Presidential Palace, though these claims were disputed.

events.title: EU Ambassador Assaulted, US Convoy Fired Upon
events.detail: EU Ambassador Aidan O'Hara was assaulted at his Khartoum home where he sheltered during fighting. The EU condemned the attack as 'a gross violation of the 1961 Vienna Convention.' A US diplomatic convoy was also fired upon; Secretary of State Blinken called the attack 'reckless.' Gen. al-Burhan declared the RSF a 'rebel group' and ordered its nationwide dissolution.

events.title: US-Saudi 72-Hour Ceasefire Immediately Violated
events.detail: A US- and Saudi-brokered 72-hour ceasefire was declared but was violated almost immediately, with fighting continuing in Khartoum and other cities. Multiple subsequent ceasefires announced throughout April and May also collapsed within hours of taking effect. Civilian evacuation efforts from Khartoum began under difficult conditions.

events.title: Foreign Nations Evacuate Nationals from Khartoum
events.detail: The US, UK, France, Germany, Saudi Arabia, and dozens of other countries evacuated their diplomatic staff and nationals from Khartoum under military escort or by air during brief pauses in fighting. Convoys were harassed and fired upon. Tens of thousands of Sudanese civilians began fleeing the capital on foot and in vehicles.

media.source: Wikipedia / Evacuation of foreign nationals during the Sudanese civil war

events.title: UN Security Council Demands Immediate Ceasefire in Sudan
events.detail: The UN Security Council unanimously adopted Resolution 2736 demanding an immediate cessation of hostilities in Sudan, humanitarian access, and protection of civilians. The resolution condemned attacks on civilians and aid workers. Neither the SAF nor RSF complied, and fighting intensified across Khartoum and Darfur through May and June 2023.

events.title: Jeddah Peace Talks Begin Under US-Saudi Mediation
events.detail: SAF and RSF envoys met in Jeddah, Saudi Arabia, under US and Saudi mediation. The talks produced several short-lived agreements on humanitarian access and protecting civilians, but fighting continued throughout. The RSF refused to evacuate Khartoum's residential neighborhoods it had seized. Multiple rounds of talks through May and June produced no lasting ceasefire.

events.title: RSF Attacks Massalit Neighborhoods in El Geneina
events.detail: Nine days after fighting broke out in Khartoum, RSF forces attacked a SAF convoy in El Geneina, West Darfur, then turned on majority Massalit neighborhoods. Over following weeks, even after Massalit armed defenders were overwhelmed, RSF and allied Arab militias systematically targeted unarmed Massalit civilians in a pattern consistent with ethnic cleansing.

events.title: West Darfur Governor Khamis Abakar Assassinated
events.detail: West Darfur Governor Khamis Abakar, who had publicly accused the RSF of genocide against the Massalit, was killed at RSF-controlled Geneina International Airport as he attempted to evacuate. His assassination was widely attributed to the RSF. Days later, Sudanese Red Crescent staff counted 2,000 bodies on Geneina's streets before being overwhelmed by the scale.

events.title: RSF Opens Fire on Civilian Evacuation Convoy in El Geneina
events.detail: RSF fighters opened fire on a kilometers-long convoy of civilians attempting to flee El Geneina. The RSF pursued, rounded up, and shot men, women, and children who ran through streets or tried to swim across the Kajja river. The UN Security Council later received a report estimating 10,000–15,000 people killed in El Geneina from April to November 2023.

events.title: RSF Consolidates Control Across Most of Khartoum
events.detail: By mid-2023, RSF forces had seized most of Khartoum's residential areas, using civilian buildings as bases and blocking SAF ground forces. The SAF responded with airstrikes on RSF positions in the capital, causing massive civilian casualties and infrastructure destruction. The historic city center, including the Republican Palace compound, remained a contested battleground.

events.title: UN Discovers Mass Grave of 87 Massalit near El Geneina
events.detail: A UN investigation discovered a mass grave of 87 individuals near El Geneina, all identified as Massalit civilians killed by the RSF between June 13 and 21. The discovery corroborated survivor accounts of systematic RSF targeting of the Massalit ethnic community. The RSF also specifically targeted doctors, lawyers, and human rights activists for killing.

events.title: Jeddah Peace Talks Officially Collapse
events.detail: The SAF suspended its participation in Jeddah peace talks after accusing the RSF of violating ceasefire commitments and continuing military advances. The collapse of the internationally mediated talks left no credible peace process in place, and the war entered a more destructive phase. Both the AU and IGAD sought to fill the mediation void but were unable to bring parties back to talks.

events.title: MSF and ICRC Suspend Operations After Aid Workers Killed
events.detail: MSF and ICRC suspended operations in multiple Sudan locations after staff were killed and convoys looted. Both RSF and SAF forces were implicated in attacks on humanitarian workers and theft of medical supplies. The suspension left hundreds of thousands without medical care in Khartoum, Darfur, and Kordofan at a critical early phase of the war.

events.title: West Darfur State Falls Entirely to RSF
events.detail: RSF achieved full control of West Darfur State by June 2023, with SAF forces surrendering, defecting, or killed. With no remaining SAF presence, the RSF and allied Arab militias faced no military opposition in continuing ethnic cleansing of the Massalit. El Geneina became the epicenter of what UN and US authorities would later designate crimes against humanity.

events.title: RSF Captures Nyala, Capital of South Darfur
events.detail: RSF forces captured Nyala, the capital of South Darfur and Sudan's second-largest city, after weeks of fighting. The fall of Nyala gave the RSF control of its second regional capital in Darfur and dominance over vast southern Darfur territory. The SAF retained control of El Fasher in North Darfur as the last SAF-held Darfuri capital.

events.title: Ardamata Massacre: Up to 2,000 Killed in El Geneina
events.detail: RSF and Janjaweed fighters massacred between 800 and 2,000 people in the Ardamata neighborhood of El Geneina. The attack was ethnically targeted, primarily against the Massalit community but also against Tama, Erenga, and other non-Arab groups. Survivors recounted executions of men and boys and mass sexual violence against women and girls. HRW and UN investigators characterized the killings as crimes against humanity.

events.title: US Declares RSF Committed Crimes Against Humanity in Darfur
events.detail: The US government formally determined that RSF and allied militias committed crimes against humanity and ethnic cleansing in Darfur. Secretary of State Blinken announced sanctions on RSF leader Hemedti and other RSF commanders. The determination stopped short of the genocide designation that would come in January 2025.

events.title: RSF Seizes Wad Madani, Capital of Gezira State
events.detail: RSF forces captured Wad Madani, the capital of Gezira State and Sudan's second most populous city. The fall of Wad Madani was strategically catastrophic for the SAF, giving RSF control of Sudan's agricultural heartland and displacing over one million people. Atrocities and looting were reported across the city in the aftermath of the RSF takeover.

events.title: RSF Begins Siege of El Fasher — Last SAF Hold in Darfur
events.detail: RSF forces encircled El Fasher, the capital of North Darfur, which sheltered 800,000 of its estimated 1.5 million population as IDPs. UN investigators warned that if El Fasher fell, RSF would be able to 'complete the genocide begun by the Janjaweed.' The SAF garrison maintained resistance, but resupply became increasingly difficult under RSF encirclement.

events.title: Famine Declared in Zamzam IDP Camp
events.detail: The IPC declared famine conditions in Zamzam camp near El Fasher — home to half a million IDPs — the first famine declaration in Sudan. MSF reported a child was dying every 2 hours in the camp. WFP reported 24.6 million Sudanese facing acute hunger and 2 million at famine risk. Sudan's humanitarian crisis became the world's largest.

events.title: UN Fact-Finding Mission: Both Sides Committed 'Appalling' Abuses
events.detail: The UN Independent Fact-Finding Mission on Sudan concluded both the SAF and RSF committed 'an appalling range of harrowing human rights violations and international crimes,' including mass rape, arbitrary arrests, torture, and deliberate targeting of civilians. The RSF was found to have used starvation as a weapon of war. The mission documented 965 mass graves across Gezira, Darfur, and Kordofan.

events.title: IPC Warns Sudan on Brink of Famine
events.detail: The Integrated Food Security Phase Classification (IPC) issued a critical warning that Sudan was on the brink of famine, with 17.7 million people in acute food insecurity. The alert cited RSF control of agricultural zones, aid access blockages, and economic collapse following Wad Madani's fall. It was the most severe pre-famine warning issued for Sudan in the modern era.

events.title: RSF Captures All Four Southern Darfur State Capitals
events.detail: By February 2024, RSF had captured the capitals of all four Darfur states except North Darfur: Nyala (South Darfur), El Geneina (West Darfur), Zalingei (Central Darfur), and Al Daein (East Darfur). Only El Fasher in North Darfur remained in SAF hands. RSF dominance over four fifths of Darfur created a contiguous RSF-held territory from Darfur through Kordofan.

events.title: RSF Announces Plans for Parallel Government
events.detail: RSF leadership gathered in Nairobi, Kenya, and signed a charter establishing the 'Government of Peace and Unity,' a parallel administration based in Nyala, South Darfur. The charter declared secularism, democracy, and a decentralized structure — at odds with the SAF's internationally recognized 'Hope Government' based in Port Sudan. Multiple African states condemned the RSF's unilateral declaration.

events.title: SAF Airstrikes Strike RSF Positions in Omdurman
events.detail: SAF conducted sustained airstrikes on RSF-held western Omdurman, targeting RSF assembly areas and command posts. The airstrikes caused significant civilian casualties due to the urban environment. Omdurman remained one of the most contested cities in Sudan, divided between SAF-controlled east and RSF-held west along the Nile.

events.title: RSF Extends Control Across Gezira State
events.detail: Following the December 2023 capture of Wad Madani, RSF extended control across Gezira State, Sudan's agricultural breadbasket. RSF imposed mandatory 'taxes' on farmers and looted harvests. Over one million Gezira residents were displaced. The loss of Gezira severely disrupted food production in Sudan's most productive farming region.

events.title: UN Sanctions Monitors Name UAE as RSF Weapons Supplier
events.detail: UN Security Council sanctions monitors named the United Arab Emirates as involved in aiding the RSF with weapons and financing. The RSF's Al Junaid corporation controls gold mines producing an estimated $860 million annually, much smuggled via the UAE. Sudan's SAF condemned UAE involvement, while Abu Dhabi denied the allegations.

media.source: Wikipedia / United Arab Emirates war crimes

events.title: Healthcare System Near-Collapse Across War Zones
events.detail: WHO and MSF documented the looting and destruction of 145+ healthcare facilities across Sudan since the war's start, particularly in Darfur and Khartoum. 65% of Darfur's population was cut off from even basic medical care. Hospitals in Khartoum and Omdurman were occupied by RSF fighters or abandoned. SAF airstrikes also damaged several facilities.

media.source: Wikipedia / Timeline of the Gaza Strip healthcare collapse

events.title: SAF Launches Coordinated Khartoum Counteroffensive
events.detail: The SAF launched a coordinated offensive across the greater Khartoum tri-city area (Khartoum, Omdurman, Bahri), supported by drone strikes and Iranian drone technology. SAF forces began reclaiming neighborhoods from RSF fighters for the first time since the war's start. The offensive marked a turning point after 18 months of RSF dominance in the capital area.

events.title: SAF Retakes Bahri and Omdurman; Expels RSF from North Khartoum
events.detail: The SAF drove RSF forces out of Omdurman and recaptured Bahri (Khartoum North), including a vital oil refinery just north of the capital. SAF gained near-total control of both cities by mid-January 2025. The SAF also recaptured significant portions of Khartoum State. These victories marked the most significant SAF territorial gains since the war's start.

events.title: US Formally Designates RSF Genocide in Darfur
events.detail: The US State Department under the Biden administration formally declared that RSF and allied militias committed acts of genocide in Darfur. US also sanctioned RSF leader Hemedti and subsequently sanctioned SAF leader Burhan for the SAF's own atrocities and obstruction of humanitarian aid. The genocide designation was the most serious international legal finding since the war began.

events.title: SAF Drone Strikes Shift Battle for Khartoum
events.detail: SAF deployed Iranian-supplied loitering munitions and Turkish TB2 drones in a sustained campaign against RSF command nodes and supply lines across the greater Khartoum area. The drone strikes targeting RSF logistics and leadership positions significantly degraded RSF effectiveness and enabled SAF ground forces to advance for the first time since war began. Analysts credited the drone campaign as a turning point in the urban battle.

events.title: SAF Recaptures Wad Madani, Gezira State Capital
events.detail: SAF forces recaptured Wad Madani, the capital of Gezira State, 13 months after its fall to the RSF in December 2023. The recapture was a major symbolic and strategic victory, restoring SAF control over Sudan's agricultural heartland. However, RSF destruction of infrastructure and displacement of over a million residents left the city severely damaged.

events.title: SAF Breaks RSF Siege of El Obeid, North Kordofan Capital
events.detail: SAF forces broke through the RSF siege of El Obeid (also known as Al-Ubayyid), the capital of North Kordofan State that had been under RSF encirclement for nearly two years. The relief of El Obeid opened a key logistics corridor and denied the RSF control of a strategically critical central Sudan hub.

events.title: SAF Announces Recapture of Khartoum Proper
events.detail: The SAF announced the recapture of Khartoum city, including the Presidential Palace, Khartoum International Airport, and army headquarters — nearly two years after losing them to the RSF at the war's start. The recapture carried enormous symbolic weight for the SAF's claim to state sovereignty, though significant pockets of RSF resistance remained in some areas.

events.title: El Fasher Falls — RSF Completes Darfur Conquest
events.detail: RSF forces captured El Fasher, the last SAF-held capital in Darfur, triggering the worst atrocity of the war. UN investigators documented ethnically targeted killings, widespread sexual violence, and enforced disappearances during the RSF takeover. The UN Fact-Finding Mission found evidence of at least three underlying acts of genocide against the Zaghawa and Fur communities. RSF fighters reportedly said: 'We want to eliminate anything black from Darfur.'

events.title: RSF Kills at Least 1,500 in El Fasher Post-Capture Massacres
events.detail: Following El Fasher's fall, the RSF killed at least 1,500 people in systematic massacres, targeting the Zaghawa and Fur ethnic communities. Humanitarian experts characterized the atrocities as the worst war crime of the entire conflict. The UN Fact-Finding Mission said the siege 'systematically weakened the targeted population through starvation, deprivation, trauma and confinement' before the final assault.

events.title: RSF Launches Full-Scale Assault on Zamzam IDP Camp
events.detail: RSF forces launched a full-scale military assault on Zamzam IDP camp near El Fasher — the largest IDP camp in Sudan with 500,000 residents. RSF attacks killed between 300 and 1,500 people, the majority women and children. WFP had already temporarily paused food distribution to the camp in February 2025. The assault cut off the last remaining food and water supplies to the camp.

events.title: UN Fact-Finding Mission: El Fasher Shows 'Hallmarks of Genocide'
events.detail: The UN Independent International Fact-Finding Mission on Sudan released findings stating that RSF actions in El Fasher and surrounding Darfur show 'hallmarks of genocide' against the Zaghawa and Fur communities. The mission said evidence establishes that at least three underlying acts of genocide were committed. The RSF has now consolidated full control over all five of Darfur's regional capitals.

events.title: WFP Temporarily Halts Food Distribution to Zamzam Camp
events.detail: The World Food Programme temporarily paused food distributions to Zamzam IDP camp — home to 500,000 people — due to RSF-imposed access restrictions and security threats against WFP staff. The pause cut food supplies to a camp already under IPC Phase 5 famine conditions. Aid organizations described the situation as catastrophic, with WFP unable to reach the population most at risk of starvation.

events.title: UN Security Council Calls for Ceasefire; Arms Embargo Blocked
events.detail: The UN Security Council passed a resolution calling for an immediate ceasefire in Sudan, but efforts to impose an arms embargo were blocked by Russia and China, citing concerns about sovereignty and the SAF government's objections. The US and European nations supported the embargo as a measure to cut RSF weapons supply. The ceasefire resolution had no enforcement mechanism and was ignored by both parties.

media.source: Wikipedia / Arms embargoes on Israel since 2023

events.title: Sudan Splits: Two Rival Governments Assert Sovereignty
events.detail: By 2025, Sudan had two rival governments: the SAF's internationally recognized 'Hope Government' in Port Sudan (eastern Sudan) and the RSF's 'Government of Peace and Unity' in Nyala, Darfur. Each was nominally civilian but effectively military-controlled, and each backed by different foreign powers — the SAF by Egypt, Iran, and Turkey; the RSF by the UAE and elements of Chad.

events.title: Battles for Kordofan Continue as War Enters Third Year
events.detail: As the Sudan conflict enters its third year, fighting continues in North and South Kordofan states, where RSF and SAF contest key towns and supply routes. The SAF, having reclaimed Khartoum and Wad Madani, seeks to push westward, while RSF—dominant in all of Darfur—aims to consolidate and resist SAF incursions. No peace negotiations are actively underway.
